summary:
The Battle of Magnesia (190 BC) was the decisive Roman victory over [[event:1050|Antiochus III the Great]] of the [[event:1041|Seleucid Empire]] that ended Seleucid power in Asia Minor and established Rome as the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean — a turning point comparable in importance to the [[event:1067|Battle of Pydna]] for Greek affairs.

Antiochus III had been expanding Seleucid power aggressively, reconquering territories in the east and pushing into Greece, where he clashed with Rome's interests. After defeating him at the Battle of Thermopylae and driving him from Greece, Rome pursued him to Asia Minor. At Magnesia (near modern Manisa, Turkey), the Roman commander Lucius Scipio — supported by his more famous brother Scipio Africanus ([[event:1228|Scipio Africanus]]) as political advisor — defeated Antiochus's numerically superior army. The subsequent Treaty of Apamea (188 BC) stripped Antiochus of all territory west of the Taurus Mountains, imposed a massive war indemnity, required surrender of war elephants, and prohibited him from operating a navy in certain waters. These terms permanently crippled Seleucid power and opened Asia Minor to Roman client kingdoms, reshaping the political map of the ancient Near East.
